summary: Gruber examines Netflix's decision to permanently stop supporting iTunes billing for new subscribers, calling it a significant development given Netflix's status as the top-grossing App Store app. He notes that Netflix has long enjoyed a preferential 85/15 revenue split with Apple, making this move even more telling about the state of Apple's relationship with content providers. His main critique centers not on Apple taking a cut of subscriptions, but on App Store rules that prevent apps from telling users how to sign up outside the app. He demonstrates the absurdity by calling Netflix support through the app and being told to visit netflix.com — information the app itself isn't allowed to display.
key_points: Netflix permanently ended iTunes billing for new users globally after testing the change in multiple markets, a major move from the App Store's top-grossing app
Netflix had a special 85/15 revenue split with Apple from the start, making the departure more significant than typical developer grievances
Apple's anti-steering rules prevent apps from even telling users in plain language that they need to sign up on the web, creating deliberate confusion
Gruber argues Apple should compete on convenience rather than relying on obfuscation to retain its cut of subscription revenue
A Netflix support rep reached through the app itself told Gruber to go to netflix.com — highlighting the absurdity of rules that forbid the app from saying the same thing
money_quotes: It really tells you how fractious Apple's relationship is with content providers if the most successful one on the platform stops supporting it.
Apple can make the rules — it's their platform. But it's just wrong that one of the rules is that apps aren't allowed to explain the rules to users.
Apple should be earning its share of in-app subscription revenue by competing on convenience, not confusion and obfuscation.
If Apple wants to insist on a cut of in-app purchased subscription revenue, that's their prerogative.
Not only is Netflix not allowed to link to their website, they can't even tell the user they need to go to netflix.com to sign up.
I told him I was using the iPad app and trying to sign up, but couldn't figure out how. He told me I need to go to netflix.com in my browser.
key_insight: Apple's App Store anti-steering rules that prevent apps from telling users how to subscribe outside the app are indefensible — Apple should win developers' participation through superior convenience, not enforced confusion.
